Proof

We only need to prove the if part. Suppose (5.1.2) holds. Call the value on both sides \(\int_{R}\, f\text{.}\) Our proof in Proposition Proposition 5.1.7 essentially carries over to show that, for any \(\epsilon >0\text{,}\) there exists some \(\delta >0\text{,}\) such that for any partition \(\cP\) of \(R\) with \(\lambda(\cP) < \delta\text{,}\) we have
\begin{equation*} \int_{R}\, f-\epsilon < L(f, \cP) \le \int_{R}\, f \le U(f, \cP) < \int_{R}\, f +\epsilon. \end{equation*}
Then for any choice of \(\bx_{\alpha}\in R_{\alpha}\in \cP\text{,}\) we have
\begin{equation*} \int_{R}\, f-\epsilon < L(f, \cP) \le R(f, \cP, \{\bx_{\alpha}\})\le U(f, \cP) < \int_{R}\, f +\epsilon, \end{equation*}
which shows that \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \(R\text{.}\)
in-context
